  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "This report is a false positive. The reported method is the interface/API `Connection.data(Map<String, String> data)`, and its concrete implementation in `HttpConnection.data(Map<String, String> data)` does not acquire or own any releasable resource. It only iterates over the caller-provided map and converts each entry into a `KeyVal` made of plain `String` key/value pairs. A `Map<String, String>` is ordinary Java heap data managed by GC, not a resource that requires explicit release under CWE-772. The real releasable resource in this API family is the `InputStream` overload, which is separately documented as requiring the caller to close it. Therefore the tool selected the wrong source/sink pair for CWE-772. There is no real leak path from this `Map` parameter to an unreleased external resource, and no realistic crash/security impact follows from calling this overload.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Interface documentation distinguishes the stream overload from the map overload. In `src/main/java/org/jsoup/Connection.java`, lines 255-257 state for `data(String key, String filename, InputStream inputStream)`: `You must close the InputStream in a finally block.` The `Map` overload at lines 282-287 only says `Adds all of the supplied data to the request data parameters` and takes `Map<String, String> data`. The implementation in `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java`, lines 246-251, is: `public Connection data(Map<String, String> data) { Validate.notNullParam(data, \"data\"); for (Map.Entry<String, String> entry : data.entrySet()) { req.data(KeyVal.create(entry.getKey(), entry.getValue())); } return this; }`. This shows the input map is only traversed; it is not stored as a resource handle and nothing needs closing. The stored request data is a `Collection<Connection.KeyVal>` (`HttpConnection.java`, lines 765-773), and `KeyVal.create(String key, String value)` builds an object containing only `String key` and `String value` (`HttpConnection.java`, lines 1343-1363). By contrast, only `KeyVal` objects created through `create(String key, String filename, InputStream stream)` carry an `InputStream` (`HttpConnection.java`, lines 1353-1355, 1390-1398). So the reported `Map` parameter is not a CWE-772 resource at all.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

In the implementation, `HttpConnection.Response.bodyStream()` either returns a `BufferedInputStream` over an in-memory `ByteArrayInputStream` when the body was already buffered, or returns a wrapped response stream and marks it read-once. Step 3: jsoup documents that the caller must close the stream, project tests use `try-with-resources`, and the close path reaches the underlying HTTP response stream. Step 4: any unreleased resource would come from application misuse of the API (caller forgets to close the returned stream), not from jsoup leaking the resource itself. Closing that stream closes `buff`, and `src/main/java/org/jsoup/internal/SimpleBufferedInput.java:151-155` closes the underlying stream and releases the buffer. Also, `SimpleBufferedInput.closeIfDone()` at `106-110` auto-closes the underlying stream on EOF. Integration tests in `src/test/java/org/jsoup/integration/ConnectIT.java:243-245`, `278-279`, and `300-320` use `try (BufferedInputStream stream = ...execute().bodyStream())`, confirming caller-owned lifetime and intended safe usage.",

  "analysis": "The report is a false positive. The reported API `Connection.KeyVal inputStream(InputStream inputStream)` is not a resource-acquisition site that owns the stream; it is a setter-style API that stores a caller-supplied `InputStream` for later upload. jsoup's public contract explicitly states that the caller must close such streams in a `finally` block. Because ownership never transfers to jsoup, the absence of a close in this method does not constitute CWE-772 in jsoup itself. A leak can only happen if application code opens a stream and then violates the documented API contract, which is an application misuse issue rather than a library vulnerability.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "1) The report points to an interface declaration, not a resource-owning implementation: `src/main/java/org/jsoup/Connection.java:1100` declares `KeyVal inputStream(InputStream inputStream);`.\n2) jsoup documents caller ownership for upload streams. In `src/main/java/org/jsoup/Connection.java:249-257`, `data(String key, String filename, InputStream inputStream)` says: caller must close the stream in a `finally` block. The overload at `:263-271` repeats this. `requestBodyStream(InputStream)` also says the caller should close the stream at `:323-333` and `:860-869`.\n3) The implementation only stores the reference. `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:1390-1393` does `this.stream = inputStream;` in `HttpConnection.KeyVal.inputStream(...)`; it does not open a resource.\n4) The request path only consumes the stream. `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:1279-1289` fetches `keyVal.inputStream()` and passes it to `DataUtil.crossStreams(input, outputStream)`. `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/DataUtil.java:197-208` explicitly documents `crossStreams` as `Doesn't close them.` This matches the caller-owned contract.\n5) Real project usage confirms this design. In `src/test/java/org/jsoup/integration/ConnectTest.java:715-727`, the test opens `FileInputStream stream = new FileInputStream(uploadFile);`, passes it to `fileData.inputStream(stream);`, executes the request, and then closes it in `finally { stream.close(); }`.\nDataflow: caller creates `FileInputStream` -> jsoup stores reference in `KeyVal` -> jsoup reads it during multipart upload -> caller closes it. There is no jsoup-internal acquisition-without-release path.",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}

  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "The report is a false positive because the reported API, `Connection.response()`, is not a resource-acquisition or resource-release point for CWE-772. Step 1 (B1): the sink/source is misidentified. In `src/main/java/org/jsoup/Connection.java`, `response()` is only an accessor declaration, and in `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java` it merely checks `res != null` and returns the already-created response object. Calling `response()` does not open a socket, allocate a stream, or transfer ownership of a new unreleased resource. The actual resource lifecycle is elsewhere: `execute()` creates the response and may attach `bodyStream`, while cleanup is performed by `parse()`, `readFully()`, and `safeClose()`. Step 2: the tool appears to rely on shallow API labeling rather than the concrete lifecycle. In real use, higher-level APIs like `get()` and `post()` immediately call `res.parse()`, which closes the underlying stream. Even when a streamed response exists, the relevant close obligations are tied to `bodyStream()` or `streamParser()`, not to `response()`. Therefore the reported method itself would not trigger CWE-772 in practice and would not realistically cause crashes or exploitable security impact as reported.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Evidence that `response()` is only an accessor: `src/main/java/org/jsoup/Connection.java:517` declares `Response response();`. Its implementation at `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:381-385` is: `if (res == null) throw new IllegalArgumentException(...); return res;`. No resource is acquired there. Actual acquisition occurs in `HttpConnection.Response.execute(...)`, where a `RequestExecutor` is created and an input stream may be assigned to `res.bodyStream` (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:889-940`). Actual release points exist: `parse()` calls `safeClose()` (`1003-1008`), `readFully()` closes in `finally` via `safeClose()` (`1033-1042`), and `safeClose()` closes `bodyStream` and then `executor.safeClose()` (`1122-1133`). High-level APIs are safe by design: `get()` and `post()` call `execute()` and then `res.parse()` (`348-360`), so the response stream is consumed and closed internally. The API docs also show the real caller-managed resource points: `bodyStream()` says 'You should close the input stream when you're done' (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/Connection.java:1042-1052`), and `streamParser()` is documented/implemented as caller-closeable (`HttpConnection.java:1012-1026`, `StreamParser.java:40-41`).",

}",

  "analysis": "This report is a false positive. The reported method `RequestExecutor.responseBody()` is only an abstract declaration, so the tool selected the wrong program point as the leak source/sink candidate. In jsoup's real execution flow, the concrete response stream is obtained in executor implementations, then handed to `HttpConnection.Response`, which either closes it on library-controlled paths (`parse()`, `readFully()`, exception handling, redirect cleanup) or intentionally transfers ownership to the caller for streaming APIs (`bodyStream()` and `streamParser()`). That means jsoup itself does not exhibit a CWE-772 resource leak here. A user could still misuse the public streaming API by not closing the returned stream/parser, but that would be caller misuse, not a vulnerability in the reported method or in jsoup's internal lifecycle management, and it is not attacker-triggerable as an internal library leak.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "`src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/RequestExecutor.java:25` is only `abstract InputStream responseBody() throws IOException;`, so it does not itself acquire or leak any resource. The real acquisition points are `UrlConnectionExecutor.responseBody()` (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/UrlConnectionExecutor.java:65-68`) and `HttpClientExecutor.responseBody()` (`src/main/java11/org/jsoup/helper/HttpClientExecutor.java:131-134`). Those streams are wrapped in `HttpConnection.Response.execute()` at `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:931-943`. Library-managed close paths exist: `parse()` calls `safeClose()` (`1003-1008`), `readFully()` closes in a `finally` block (`1033-1042`), exceptions close via `if (res != null) res.safeClose();` (`947-949`), redirects close previous responses via `previousResponse.safeClose()` (`1145-1152`), and `Response.safeClose()` closes `bodyStream` then `executor.safeClose()` (`1122-1133`). For the two streaming APIs, ownership is explicitly transferred to callers rather than leaked: `Connection.Response.bodyStream()` documents `You should close the input stream when you're done with it.` (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/Connection.java:1042-1052`), and `HttpConnection.Response.streamParser()` states the caller must close the parser (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:1025-1026`); `StreamParser.close()` releases the underlying reader/resources (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/parser/StreamParser.java:160-167`). Tests also use try-with-resources for these APIs (`src/test/java/org/jsoup/integration/ConnectIT.java:243-244,278` and `src/test/java/org/jsoup/integration/ConnectTest.java:304-304`).",

",
  "response": "{
  "judgement": "FP",
  "analysis": "This is a false positive. Step 1: the reported source/sink is misidentified. `Connection.newRequest()` does not acquire a socket, stream, file handle, or other releasable external resource; it only creates a new `HttpConnection` wrapper that copies request/session settings. The jsoup API documentation explicitly states that a socket connection is only made when `execute()`, `get()`, or `post()` is called, not when `newRequest()` is called. Therefore, even if the returned `conn` object is never used again, that does not by itself trigger CWE-772. Step 2: the tool also appears to rely on shallow context, because the report is attached to the interface declaration `Connection newRequest();` rather than the implementation and real acquisition path. Step 3: in the real execution path, the actual network/body resources are acquired later in `Response.execute(...)` and are closed on normal and error paths by `Response.safeClose()` plus executor-specific cleanup. Step 4: there is no prompt/CWE deviation needed to explain this; the main problem is that the tool treated a plain Java session/request object as a resource requiring explicit release.",
  "FP_Reason": ["B1"],
  "evidence": "Evidence that `newRequest()` is not a resource acquisition point: `src/main/java/org/jsoup/Connection.java:38-40` says the `Connection` object does not hold a long-lived server connection and that a socket is only made at `execute()`, `get()`, or `post()`. The implementation at `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:125-127` is only `return new HttpConnection(req);`. The copy constructor path at `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:648-666` copies configuration fields and shares the cookie manager; it does not open any stream/socket. The real acquisition happens later in `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:888-940`, where `RequestDispatch.get(req, prevRes)` executes the HTTP request and `executor.responseBody()` returns the response stream. Those resources are then released by `Response.safeClose()` at `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:1122-1133`, which closes `bodyStream` and calls `executor.safeClose()`. Executor cleanup is implemented in `src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/UrlConnectionExecutor.java:71-75` via `disconnect()` and in `src/main/java11/org/jsoup/helper/HttpClientExecutor.java:136-145` via closing the response body stream. Typical jsoup usage also auto-closes: `get()`/`post()` call `execute()` and then `res.parse()` (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:348-360`), and `parse()` calls `safeClose()` (`src/main/java/org/jsoup/helper/HttpConnection.java:1003-1008`).",
  "new_reason:": ""
}"
}
